lead_title: Agentic architecture quality — orchestration, memory, and context — is the real determinant of AI product performance, not raw model capability

options.conviction: L2: Founders optimizing for cost by downgrading models are solving the wrong problem — smart orchestration with cheaper models beats expensive models running solo
options.post:
Everyone's debating which model to upgrade to.

Wrong question.

Ethan Mollick just proved cheaper models with smart orchestration outperform expensive models running solo.

The mechanism works through division of cognitive labor.

At Atlan, when we build agents, we don't have them click buttons. They call APIs, read from databases, talk to other apps through MCPs.

The orchestrator handles the hard stuff - planning, quality control, routing complex tasks to appropriate specialists. Cheap models execute under supervision.

Teams spending on model upgrades instead of orchestration architecture are optimizing the wrong axis.

Real implementations look like GPT-4 orchestrating multiple Claude Haiku instances. Or Sonnet coordinating with specialized models for code generation, data analysis, and content creation.

The routing logic becomes the critical engineering challenge:
- Task type classification
- Estimated difficulty scoring
- Error history tracking
- Quality gates between models

Most teams are building agents like single-player games. One model doing everything. But the winning teams are building orchestras.

In practice, this means your expensive model becomes the conductor, not the first violin. It decides which cheaper models handle specific subtasks, validates outputs, and manages the overall workflow.

The cost savings compound quickly. Instead of paying premium rates for every operation, you pay premium rates only for coordination and quality control.

Teams optimizing AI costs by simply downgrading models are missing the architectural opportunity.

The winning pattern is smart orchestration with validated handoffs between model tiers.

What's your current model hierarchy looking like?

options.conviction: L1: Teams focusing on better models while ignoring memory systems don't realize memory can actually make models worse
options.post:
I've been watching teams add memory to their AI agents.

Every week I see the same pattern.

"Our agent needs to remember past conversations."

So they build memory storage. Raw conversation logs. Previous interactions. User preferences.

Then performance gets worse.

New research shows memory systems degrade model performance by 15% after 50 interactions. By 200 interactions, agreement with false statements jumps 40%.

The mechanism is context pollution.

Models optimize for consistency with stored context rather than accuracy. Users get responses that feel personalized but are less truthful.

At Atlan, we learned this building Jake, our AI SDR. Memory systems require the same careful engineering as model orchestration.

We separate preference data from factual data. We implement memory decay. We add validation layers that fact-check stored information before retrieval.

Teams adding memory without considering degradation effects are unknowingly making their products worse.

Simon Willison's new agentic engineering guide covers this exact problem. The compound engineering loop concept - agents improve through feedback cycles, not just memory accumulation.

Most agent implementations skip the feedback mechanisms and just store everything.

That's how you get agents that remember your biases instead of helping you think better.

What does your team's memory validation look like?

options.conviction: L3: Enterprise context management just became a real market with Jedify's $24M raise — teams building internal context solutions are about to get outgunned
options.post:
Someone raised $24M just to babysit AI context.

Jedify. With Snowflake Ventures participating.

Most teams I talk to are still building context pipelines as side projects.

"We'll just connect our agent to Slack and the database. How hard could it be?"

Six months later, they're drowning in context management.

Enterprise agents need business-specific data sources. Context consistency across different systems. Audit trails for agent decisions. Integration with data warehouses.

The funding validates what practitioners already knew - enterprise context management exceeds what most teams want to build internally.

Dedicated context companies now have more resources, specialized expertise, and strategic partnerships.

Teams that thought they could build context pipelines as a weekend hack are competing against dedicated companies with $24M in funding.

This changes the build-versus-buy equation for agent infrastructure:
- Context becomes a competitive advantage for teams that solve it well
- A liability for teams that handle it poorly
- A vendor category for everyone else

The patterns that worked for early agent implementations don't scale to enterprise requirements.

Generic AI agents lack the business context needed to make good decisions in enterprise environments.

Are you still building your context layer in-house?
